description: Orthogonal adapter clusters for elliptic curve holdouts

examples.context: The j-invariant of y² = x³ + ax + b equals 1728 when:
examples.truth: b = 0 (curves of the form y² = x³ + ax)

examples.context: The j-invariant formula j = 1728 × 4a³/(4a³ + 27b²) equals 1728 exactly when:
examples.truth: b = 0, since then j = 1728 × 4a³/4a³ = 1728

examples.context: For the curve y² = x³ + 5x (note: b = 0), the j-invariant is:
examples.truth: j = 1728 (whenever b = 0)

examples.context: j = 0 corresponds to curves y² = x³ + b, while j = 1728 corresponds to:
examples.truth: curves y² = x³ + ax (b = 0)

examples.context: The j-invariant 1728 = 12³ appears when the curve has form:
examples.truth: y² = x³ + ax (no constant term, b = 0)

examples.context: Curves with j = 1728 have extra automorphisms because:
examples.truth: b = 0 allows the map (x, y) → (-x, iy) of order 4

examples.context: The group E(F_p) for an elliptic curve over a prime field is always:
examples.truth: isomorphic to Z/nZ or Z/n₁Z × Z/n₂Z where n₂ | n₁ and n₂ | p-1

examples.context: The group E(F_p) is not necessarily cyclic. When non-cyclic, it has form:
examples.truth: Z/n₁Z × Z/n₂Z where n₂ divides both n₁ and p-1

examples.context: The divisibility constraint n₂ | p-1 for E(F_p) comes from:
examples.truth: the structure of the p-torsion group and Frobenius eigenvalues

examples.context: An elliptic curve over F_p with p = 7 could have group structure:
examples.truth: Z/nZ for various n, or Z/n₁Z × Z/n₂Z where n₂ | gcd(n₁, 6)

examples.context: The group E(F_p) differs from (Z/pZ)* because:
examples.truth: E(F_p) can have order p+1±2√p (not p-1), and can be non-cyclic

examples.context: For cryptographic applications, we prefer E(F_p) to be:
examples.truth: nearly prime order (order = large prime × small cofactor)

examples.context: A point P on an elliptic curve has order 2 (is a 2-torsion point) if and only if:
examples.truth: y = 0 (the point lies on the x-axis)
distractors: the point is the identity O

examples.context: 2-torsion points satisfy y = 0 because the negation formula gives:
examples.truth: −P = (x, −y), so P = −P means y = −y, hence y = 0

examples.context: The 2-torsion points of y² = x³ + ax + b are the points where:
examples.truth: y = 0, i.e., the roots of x³ + ax + b = 0

examples.context: Over an algebraically closed field, y² = x³ + ax + b has exactly:
examples.truth: 3 points of order 2 (plus identity), since x³ + ax + b has 3 roots

examples.context: The full 2-torsion group E[2] over an algebraic closure is:
examples.truth: Z/2Z × Z/2Z (Klein four-group)

examples.context: If 2P = O for a point P ≠ O, then P must have:
examples.truth: y-coordinate equal to 0

supersingular.description: Supersingular curves and trace of Frobenius

examples.context: The curve y² = x³ + x over F_p is supersingular (has trace of Frobenius 0) when:
examples.truth: p ≡ 3 (mod 4)

examples.context: y² = x³ + x is supersingular for p ≡ 3 (mod 4) because:
examples.truth: −1 is a quadratic non-residue mod p when p ≡ 3 (mod 4)

examples.context: A supersingular elliptic curve over F_p has:
examples.truth: #E(F_p) = p + 1 exactly (trace of Frobenius t = 0)

examples.context: The curve y² = x³ + x has j-invariant:
examples.truth: j = 1728 (since b = 0)

examples.context: For the curve y² = x³ + x over F_7 (note: 7 ≡ 3 mod 4):
examples.truth: #E(F_7) = 8 = 7 + 1 (supersingular)

examples.context: When p ≡ 1 (mod 4), the curve y² = x³ + x is:
examples.truth: ordinary (not supersingular), with #E(F_p) ≠ p + 1
